questions.question: What are the four types of digital boundaries described in the lesson?

options.text: Time (when you're available), Notification (what gets your attention), Social (who has access to you), Informational (what you consume)
Email, Social Media, Phone, Computer
Work, Family, Friends, Entertainment
Morning, Afternoon, Evening, Night

questions.explanation: The four types of digital boundaries are: (1) Time boundaries (when you're available — protects rest, focus, family time), (2) Notification boundaries (what gets your attention — protects focus and peace of mind), (3) Social boundaries (who has access to you — protects emotional energy and privacy), and (4) Informational boundaries (what you consume — protects mental health and values alignment). Each type addresses a different form of digital intrusion and requires specific strategies.

questions.question: What is 'technoference' and how does it affect relationships?

options.text: Technoference is using technology to improve relationships through better communication

Technoference is technology interference in face-to-face interactions (phones at dinner, during conversations, in bed) — research shows it reduces relationship quality, empathy, and perceived closeness
Technoference only affects romantic relationships, not friendships or family
Technoference is a myth created by anti-technology activists

questions.explanation: Technoference is when phones interrupt face-to-face interactions — partner on phone during dinner, kids ignored while parents scroll, divided attention masquerading as presence. Research shows phone presence during interactions reduces relationship quality, empathy, and perceived closeness. Partners report feeling unimportant, ignored, or competing with a device. The mere presence of a phone (even face-down) reduces cognitive capacity in conversations. Intimacy erodes when presence is fragmented.

questions.question: What does research on work-family boundary permeability show?

options.text: Boundary permeability improves work-life balance by increasing flexibility

Stronger role boundaries (clear separation between work and personal domains) protect against burnout — when work intrudes into personal time, stress increases and wellbeing decreases, especially in remote work
Boundary permeability has no effect on mental health
Only people with anxiety disorders are affected by boundary permeability

questions.explanation: Work-family boundary permeability research shows that when work intrudes into personal time (checking email at night, working weekends, thinking about work during family time), stress increases and wellbeing decreases. Stronger role boundaries — clear separation between work and personal domains — protect against burnout. Your nervous system never fully recovers if you're always 'on call.' Remote work amplifies this problem because physical separation has collapsed (no commute, no office door to close). This requires intentional boundary reconstruction.

questions.question: Which digital boundary strategy is most effective for preventing work messages from intruding into personal time?

options.text: Respond immediately to all messages to prevent them from piling up
Turn off work app notifications after defined hours (e.g., 6pm) and/or set auto-responses clarifying your availability window and providing emergency contact alternative
Feel guilty and anxious all evening but don't respond
Check messages constantly but only respond to some of them

questions.explanation: The most effective strategy is: Turn off work app notifications after your defined availability window (e.g., after 6pm) and respond during work hours. Even better: Set an auto-response clarifying your availability ('I check messages during work hours 9am–5pm. If urgent, call me.'). This trains others to respect your off-hours, protects your wellbeing, and prevents passive-aggressive resentment. If your manager needs real-time response after hours, that's a conversation about expectations — not a demand you must accommodate silently.

questions.question: When does social media consumption warrant professional mental health support?

options.text: Any social media use is pathological and requires therapy

When passive scrolling creates chronic anxiety/depression, when comparison spirals are uncontrollable despite curation efforts, or when use interferes with daily functioning (sleep, work, relationships) — indicating need for professional assessment and support
Never — social media issues can always be solved by deleting apps
Only if you spend more than 8 hours per day on social media

questions.explanation: Social media consumption warrants professional support when: passive scrolling consistently worsens mood (anxiety, depression) despite curation efforts (unfollowing comparison-triggering accounts), when you can't control the behavior despite negative consequences (compulsive use), when it interferes with daily functioning (sacrificing sleep, work performance, or real relationships), or when FOMO/comparison creates chronic feelings of inadequacy. Heavy social media use is correlated with increased anxiety and depression, particularly among adolescents. If digital boundary strategies (app limits, deletion, curation) aren't sufficient, professional assessment can identify underlying issues (anxiety disorder, depression, ADHD) that require treatment.

title: Digital Boundaries — Connected World Peace - Quiz
